title: Free the States Statement on Remaining Pro-Life Bills on General Order
author: James Silberman
orientation: Free the States surveys the five pro-life bills sitting on Oklahoma's general order in April 2022, arguing that each one substitutes performance and political theater for the bill that would have actually abolished abortion. James Silberman walks through SB1552, SJR37, SB1553, SB1503, and HB4327, exposing them as variations on incrementalism, trigger legislation, or Texas-style civil enforcement that still tolerates the murder of preborn children.
audiences: Constitutional conservative skeptical of judicial supremacy
Christian pastor discipling on abortion policy

questions.question: Why does Free the States dismiss the Oklahoma legislature's recent pro-life output as theater rather than substantive action?
questions.answer: Silberman frames the entire 2022 Oklahoma slate as politicians staging the appearance of activity while refusing to actually abolish abortion. He argues that updating the terms of existing pro-life programs gives lawmakers a fresh ribbon-cutting every session without ever costing the abortion industry a single life.
questions.quote: A favorite tactic of politicians is looking like they are doing something without actually doing anything.

questions.question: What is the history behind SB1552 and the Choosing Childbirth Act that makes it a recurring photo-op for legislators?
questions.answer: The Choosing Childbirth Act of 2017 directed grants to pregnancy centers but went unfunded until 2019. Each subsequent tweak to the funding mechanism, including SB1552's contracting amendment, becomes another opportunity for the same legislators to claim a pro-life victory.
questions.quote: Each time they update the terms of how these funds are allocated is a new pro-life bill and victory to tout. That's how the game is played.

questions.question: Why single out Senate Pro Tem Greg Treat as the author of SB1552?
questions.answer: Silberman ties Treat's authorship of a cosmetic pro-life bill to his broader record of obstructing abolition in Oklahoma, treating the bill as evidence of the same delay tactics he has used for years.
questions.quote: The author of this bill is none other than Senate Pro Tem Greg Treat whose game is delaying the abolition of abortion in Oklahoma.

questions.question: What does SJR37 actually do, and why is its protective effect uncertain?
questions.audience: Constitutional conservative skeptical of judicial supremacy
questions.answer: SJR37 would put a constitutional amendment on the ballot declaring that nothing in Oklahoma's constitution secures or protects a right to abortion. Silberman concedes it would not abolish abortion and could only possibly forestall the Oklahoma Supreme Court from inventing such a right later.
questions.quote: This would not abolish abortion, but might prevent the Oklahoma Supreme Court from attempting to read a right to abortion into our State Constitution should they ever desire to.

questions.question: How does Bradley Pierce's Dobbs amicus brief reframe the strategy of constitutional amendments as a response to Roe-style rulings?
questions.audience: Constitutional conservative skeptical of judicial supremacy
questions.answer: Pierce argues that legalized abortion is the fruit of judges ignoring the Constitution in the first place, so amending the document only invites more of the same disregard. Worse, the very act of amending in response concedes that judges may rewrite the Constitution unless the people repair it for them.
questions.quote: The very attempt to amend the Constitution to undo a judicially created 'amendment' would likely further concede the legitimacy of the concept of judicial supremacy, which is a fundamental underlying problem.

questions.question: What does Silberman say should happen if Oklahoma justices ever invent a right to abortion in the state constitution?
questions.audience: Constitutional conservative skeptical of judicial supremacy
questions.answer: Rather than relying on amendments, he insists the political branches must check rogue judges directly through impeachment or by simply refusing to enforce illegitimate rulings, with or without SJR37 on the books.
questions.quote: with or without the amendment, if Oklahoma Supreme Court justices ever attempt to do so, they should be impeached or ignored.

questions.question: Why is SB1553's 30-day homicide threshold treated as just another trigger bill?
questions.answer: SB1553 would charge abortionists with homicide for abortions performed after 30 days, but it carries the same broad health exception as SB612 and refuses to defy Roe v. Wade, leaving its enforcement contingent on federal courts.
questions.quote: Like SB612, it does not defy Roe v. Wade and thus is essentially a trigger bill.

questions.question: What makes SB1503 a near-copy of the Texas heartbeat bill, and why does its timing seem absurd?
questions.answer: SB1503 imports Texas's civil-lawsuit enforcement model targeting abortionists who kill children after six weeks. Silberman finds it strange that legislators would advance a six-week ban moments after passing a supposed total ban, but notes Oklahoma has produced contradictory bills before.
questions.quote: This bill mimics the Texas heartbeat bill by allowing for lawsuits against abortionists who murder children after six weeks.

questions.question: How does HB4327 compare to the Texas civil-enforcement law, both better and worse?
questions.answer: HB4327 improves on Texas by defining life as beginning at conception rather than at a detectable heartbeat, but it regresses by carving out exceptions for children conceived in rape or incest in addition to a life-of-the-mother exception.
questions.quote: It is better than the Texas bill in that it defines life as beginning at conception instead of a heartbeat, but it is worse than the Texas bill in that it protects the murder of children conceived in rape or incest, in addition to the exception for the life of the mother.

questions.question: What does Free the States say is the only acceptable path forward for the 2022 Oklahoma session?
questions.answer: Silberman calls on legislators to amend one of these general-order bills into a true abolition bill and rush the result through conference committee. Anything less means another year of disobedience and bloodshed under the legislature's watch.
questions.quote: Unless they amend one of these bills on general order to become an abolition bill and do whatever they have to do to hurry the amended version through conference committee, the Oklahoma legislature will have gone another year of disobeying Christ and overseeing the death of their preborn neighbors.

questions.question: How does the article frame the spiritual stakes of the legislature's choice for a Christian audience?
questions.audience: Christian pastor discipling on abortion policy
questions.answer: By naming the legislature's failure as disobedience to Christ rather than mere policy timidity, Silberman casts the lawmakers as accountable to a higher authority for the lives lost while they delay, not just to voters or to the courts.
questions.quote: the Oklahoma legislature will have gone another year of disobeying Christ and overseeing the death of their preborn neighbors.

related.title: Oklahoma's Latest "Abortion Ban" (SB612) Isn't Worth the Paper It's Printed On
related.reason: Silberman links to this companion piece in the opening paragraph; it lays out the case that the supposed total ban already passed is itself a trigger bill.

related.title: Abolitionist Perspective on the Texas Heartbeat Bill: Three Quick Observations
related.reason: SB1503 and HB4327 are explicitly modeled on the Texas heartbeat bill, and this article supplies the abolitionist critique referenced through the Liberator Podcast call-out.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Provides the underlying framework Silberman draws on when dismissing each of the five general-order bills as incrementalist theater rather than abolition.

related.title: Abolitionist Lawyer to File Amicus Brief in SCOTUS Abortion Case
related.reason: Backstory on Bradley Pierce's Dobbs amicus brief, which Silberman quotes when critiquing SJR37 and judicial supremacy.

related.title: The Inexhaustible Cowardice of Oklahoma Senate President Greg Treat
related.reason: Establishes Greg Treat's long pattern of obstructing abolition, the same pattern Silberman cites when calling out his authorship of SB1552.

related.title: OK Legislature Sends 3 Useless Pro-Life Bills to Governor Stitt
related.reason: A 2021 precedent piece on the same legislative theater pattern, helpful for readers who want to see the recurring annual cycle this 2022 statement is responding to.

related.title: Tennessee Fetal Heartbeat Bill—A Step in the Wrong Direction
related.reason: Extends the abolitionist critique of heartbeat-style bills like SB1503 and HB4327 to a parallel state context.
